coredata.dc:title: Trophic transfer and environmental safety of carbon dots from microalgae to Daphnia
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yanhua
Ma, Yunfeng
Chen, Meilin
Zhou, Tianhan
Ji, Rong
Guo, Ruixin
Chen, Jianqiu
coredata.dc:description: The application of carbon dots (CDs), a novel carbon nanomaterial, is extensive, leading to inevitable CD pollution. However, studies on their environmental fate and related risks to aquatic ecosystems are limited. Here, the trophic transfer of CDs from Chlorella pyrenoidosa to Daphnia magna and their toxic effects on the two organisms were analyzed. 14C-labelling was used to quantify and evaluate the fate of CDs. The results showed that the radioactivity of CDs in water was >80 % of the initial radioactivity, and that water extractable residues were dominant in organisms, with only 3 % or less recovered from the mineralization product 14CO2. The distribution of radioactivity illustrated how the exposure routes changed the fate of CDs in aquatic environments. CD aggregates were found in algal cells and Daphnia intestinal tract, indicating the cellular uptake of CDs in these aquatic organisms. Wall-membrane detachment, cell collapse, and rupture were observed in the ultrastructural investigations of microalgae, whereas pneumatosis cystoides intestinalis was observed in the ultrastructural investigations of D. magna. CD exposure affected the growth and chlorophyll content of C. pyrenoidosa as well as the feeding behavior, oxidative stress system, digestive system, and symbiotic bacteria of D. magna. The toxicity of CDs is also affected by the route of exposure. These findings suggest that dietary exposure to CDs was more likely to cause environmental risk and adverse effects than aqueous exposure, and the environmental risks associated with CDs should not be underestimated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aquatic ecosystem
Cellular uptake
Environment fate and safety
Biological responses

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of radioactivity in water phase
Analysis of D. magna and microalgae samples
Response of D. magna for CDs exposure
Response of C. pyrenoidosa for CDs exposure
Responses of D. magna to CDs
Damage in oxidative stress and the digestive system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: College Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21876207 ), Double First-Class University Project ( CPU2018GY21, 24 ), and College Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The changes and distribution of 14C‑carbon from CDs during 96 h of microalgae/D. magna exposure, (A: 0.15 mg/L CDs group; B: 1.5 mg/L CDs group; C: 15 mg/L CDs group). The changes and distribution of 14C‑carbon from CDs during 96 h of exposure (D: enrichment by algae only via the aqueous route; E: enrichment by D. magna only via the aqueous route; F: enrichment by algae-D. magna via dietary route). For waterborne exposure, D. magna were exposed to polluted solution with 15 mg/L CDs. For dietary exposure, D. magna were fed 1.0 × 106 cells/mL polluted C. pyrenoidosa, and the solution was pollutant free. The data are reported as the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3).

Ultrastructural changes of C. pyrenoidosa and intestinal tract of D. magna by CDs. A, E: control group; B, F: 0.15 mg/L of CDs; C, G: 1.5 mg/L of CDs; and D, H: 15 mg/L of CDs. White arrows pointed to CD aggregates; red arrows pointed to the detachment of cell wall and membrane; green arrow pointed to cell collapse; blue arrows pointed to cell rupture; and red circles pointed to pneumatosis cystoides intestinalis.

The filtration rate (A) and ingestion rate (B) of D. magna during 96 h of exposure by the aqueous route, and the filtration rate (C), and ingestion rate (D) of D. magna during 96 h of exposure by the dietary route. The data are reported as the mean ± standard deviation (n = 5). *: significant difference between treatment groups and control group (p < 0.05); **: extremely significant difference between treatment groups and control group (p < 0.01).

Enzyme activity and gene expression in D. magna after 96 h of exposuer by the aqueous route and dietary route, respectively. A: the enzyme activity of SOD (U/mg prot.) and CAT (U/mg prot.); B: the gene expression of SOD and CAT; C: the enzyme activity of lipase (U/mg prot.), trypsin (×104 U/mg prot.), and α-amylase (U/mg prot.); D: the gene expression of lipase, trypsin, and α-amylase. The enzyme activity data are reported as the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3), and the gene expression data are reported as the mean ± standard deviation (n = 6, three biological repeats and two technical repeats). *: significant difference between treatment groups and control group (p < 0.05); **: extremely significant difference between treatment groups and control group (p < 0.01).

(A) Main bacterial phyla and their relative abundances of mutualistic microbiota in D. magna; (B) Taxonomic classification of mutualistic microbiota at the genus level. The data are reported as the mean (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Trophic transfer and environmental safety of carbon dots from microalgae to Daphnia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, School of the Environment, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Pollution Control and Resource Reuse, School of the Environment, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Engineering, China Pharmaceutical University, 210009 Nanjing, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yanhua Liu and Yunfeng Ma contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of carbon dots (CDs), a novel carbon nanomaterial, is extensive, leading to inevitable CD pollution. However, studies on their environmental fate and related risks to aquatic ecosystems are limited. Here, the trophic transfer of CDs from Chlorella pyrenoidosa to Daphnia magna and their toxic effects on the two organisms were analyzed. 14C-labelling was used to quantify and evaluate the fate of CDs. The results showed that the radioactivity of CDs in water was >80 % of the initial radioactivity, and that water extractable residues were dominant in organisms, with only 3 % or less recovered from the mineralization product 14CO2. The distribution of radioactivity illustrated how the exposure routes changed the fate of CDs in aquatic environments. CD aggregates were found in algal cells and Daphnia intestinal tract, indicating the cellular uptake of CDs in these aquatic organisms. Wall-membrane detachment, cell collapse, and rupture were observed in the ultrastructural investigations of microalgae, whereas pneumatosis cystoides intestinalis was observed in the ultrastructural investigations of D. magna. CD exposure affected the growth and chlorophyll content of C. pyrenoidosa as well as the feeding behavior, oxidative stress system, digestive system, and symbiotic bacteria of D. magna. The toxicity of CDs is also affected by the route of exposure. These findings suggest that dietary exposure to CDs was more likely to cause environmental risk and adverse effects than aqueous exposure, and the environmental risks associated with CDs should not be underestimated.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a category of novel carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) with excellent physicochemical properties, are widely used as sensors, bioimaging agents, food preservatives, photocatalysts, and drug carriers (Zhang et al., 2020a; Alayan et al., 2020). However, more in-depth studies on CDs have found that they are not as safe as previously thought. For example, after exposure to 200 μg/mL CDs, the survival rate of human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) was 40 % (Yang et al., 2009). When the concentration of CDs was increased to 1.0 mg/mL, the survival rate of human hepatoma cells (HepG2) decreased to 75 % (Ray et al., 2009). High concentrations of CDs have toxic effects on other mammals as well. After exposure to 0.2–20 mg/kg CDs, the serum triglyceride, serum urea, albumin, and blood sugar levels of mice changed (Wang et al., 2013). High concentrations of CDs are toxic to bacteria, animals, plants, and humans (Montes et al., 2017; Sun et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2017). Even at low concentrations, the cell growth-promoting properties of CDs can lead to an imbalance in environmental homeostasis (Agathokleous et al., 2019).

With the widespread application of CDs, they inevitably enter the natural environment through production, utilization, abandonment, and recycling (Chen et al., 2022). Because of the high water solubility of CDs, bodies of water may be important CD reservoirs, and their potential ecological impact on aquatic environments must be studied further (Chousidis et al., 2020; Kupryianchyk et al., 2011). In aquatic environments, CDs show toxicity to certain aquatic organisms (Xiao et al., 2016a, 2016b). When exposed to CD concentration of 2.5 mg/mL, the survival rate of zebrafish embryos (24 h post fertilization) decreased to 55 % (Kang et al., 2015). CDs pose apparent developmental toxicity to the embryos/larvae of Gobiocypris rarus (Xiao et al., 2016a, 2016b). CDs inhibit photosynthesis and nutrient absorption in Scenedesmus obliquus (Yao et al., 2018). Additionally, the photodegraded products of CDs can cause cytotoxicity in organisms (Liu et al., 2021). Therefore, the environmental effects of CDs must be reassessed.

The microalgae Chlorella pyrenoidosa is a primary producer, and the zooplankton Daphnia magna is a filter feeder that feeds on microalgae and bacteria. When exposed to CDs, microalgae can enrich CDs in wastewater through processes like adsorption and endocytosis (Lee and Lei, 2019). The enrichment of CDs by D. magna occurs via two pathways (Liu et al., 2019a; Yu et al., 2018): water filtration and ingestion of polluted microalgae. Our previous studies have found that microalgae can transfer pollutants to more advanced organisms through the food chain. For example, 14C-labeled BPA enters rotifers, and 14C-labeled BDE-47 enters D. magna from microalgae (Guo et al., 2017; Liu et al., 2019b). Thus, similar to conventional organic pollutants, aquatic organisms can be exposed to CDs via aqueous and dietary routes. Additionally, the route-regulated toxic effects of the pollutants were demonstrated. Compared with waterborne exposure, CuO nanoparticles pose a higher risk to Lymnaea stagnalis through dietborne õexposure (Croteau et al., 2014). Therefore, the transfer of CDs among aquatic organisms at different trophic levels is crucial for risk assessment.

In this study, C. pyrenoidosa and D. magna were used as model organisms to construct the study system, focusing on the fate and potential risks of CDs in aquatic environments. Therefore, it is necessary to establish an effective method for accurately quantifying CDs in complex biological systems. 14C radioisotope tracing is an effective method for tracing and quantifying pollutants in organisms (Chen et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2017). This method traces the change in target substances by observing or measuring the intensity or behavior of radioisotope tracer atoms. In this study, 14C tracer technology was used to track the CDs from complex biological backgrounds. The three specific goals of this study were to (1) confirm the fate of CDs from microalgae to D. magna via different exposure routes, (2) identify the route-regulated toxic effects of CDs, and (3) evaluate the biosafety and risks of CDs in aquatic environments.

CDs were synthesized in our laboratory via a one-step hydrothermal method using glucose as the precursor. Glucose (0.2 g) was dissolved in 40 mL of ultrapure water, and the solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave. The reaction was conducted at 200 °C for 8 h and the crude product was obtained after naturally cooling to room temperature. The crude reaction solution was concentrated to approximately 2 mL and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000) for 12 h. Finally, the samples were freeze-dried to obtain a solid powder of CDs. The CDs were characterized using fluorescence spectroscopy (Fluoromax-4c fluorescence spectrometer, Horiba JY, France), UV–visible spectroscopy (UV-2100 spectrophotometer, Shimadzu, Japan), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Iraffinity-1S infrared spectrometer, Shimadzu, Japan), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Phi 5000 Versaprobe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, Kanagawa, Japan), and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2F20 high-resolution transmission electron microscope, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA). The prepared CDs were spherical in shape with good dispersion, and the average size was approximately 40 nm, with optimum excitation and emission wavelengths of 350 and 444 nm, respectively. Detailed characterization results are shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information).
14C-CDs were synthesized using 14C-labeled glucose as the precursor. The specific activity of 14C-CDs was 13.5 mCi/mmol.

Chlorella pyrenoidosa (FACHB-28) was purchased from the Freshwater Algae Culture Collection of the Institute of Hydrobiology, China. Algae were incubated in BG-11 medium in an artificial illumination incubator at 25 ± 1 °C and a light: dark cycle of 16 h: 8 h. The freshwater zooplankton D. magna was maintained in our laboratory and incubated in moderately hard water with C. pyrenoidosa as the food source. D. magna was cultured in medium-hardness recombinant water with a total hardness of 90–100 mg/L (CaCO3 content) and pH of 7.3–7.7. The culture light cycle was 16 h: 8 h (light: dark), culture temperature was 20.0 ± 1.0 °C, and medium was renewed 2–3 times a week.

For exposure of the feeding-affected aqueous route, C. pyrenoidosa and D. magna were used to construct a mixed ecosystem to study the fate of CDs. To avoid the influence of light on microalgae and CDs, the experiment was conducted in 50 mL glass tubes in darkness at 25 ± 1 °C in a greenhouse. Appropriate amounts of 14C-CD and non-labeled CDs, microalgae, and adult Daphnia (7 d old) were added to each tube. The total volume of the D. magna culture medium in each tube was 20 mL, initial radioactive concentration of 14C-CDs was 10,000 DPM/mL, initial chemical concentrations of CDs were 0.15, 1.5, and 15 ppm, initial density of algae was approximately 1.0 × 106 cells/mL, and number of D. magna was 20. A 6 mL vial (containing 2 mL of a 1 M NaOH solution) was suspended on the rubber stopper to capture 14CO2. Each group consisted of three replicates. Sampling was conducted at 0, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h to detect the amount of mineralization and distribution of 14C-CDs in water, microalgae, and D. magna. Microalgae were supplemented every 24 h to an initial concentration of 1.0 × 106 cells/mL. Chlorella pyrenoidosa and D. magna were exposed to non-labeled CDs for 96 h to observe their structures. Chlorella pyrenoidosa and Daphnia were then fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde and processed following the standard protocol for ultrathin sections using an ultramicrotome (Leica EM UC7, Germany). Ultrathin sections were examined using TEM.

The experimental design for the waterborne exposure used the following method: A total of 1.0 × 106 cells/mL microalgae or 20 adult Daphnia were added to 20 mL of the culture solution with a 10,000 DPM/mL 14C-CD solution and 15 ppm CD chemical concentration. A 6 mL vial (containing 2 mL of a 1 M NaOH solution) was suspended on the rubber stopper to capture 14CO2. Each group consisted of three replicates. All experimental devices were cultured under the same conditions as those used in the mixed ecosystem experiments. Sampling was conducted at 0, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h to detect the amount of mineralization and distribution of 14C-CDs in water, C. pyrenoidosa, and D. magna. Daphnia magna was not fed during the experiment.

The experimental design for the dietary exposure used the following method: C. pyrenoidosa exposed to 14C-CDs for 48 h was separated by centrifugation (4000 rpm, 10 min) and suspended in 20 mL of CD-free culture solution to be fed to D. magna. Each tube included 1.0 × 106 cells/mL of polluted C. pyrenoidosa and 20 D. magna. A 6 mL vial (containing 2 mL of 1 M NaOH solution) was suspended on the rubber stopper to capture 14CO2. All experiments were conducted under the same conditions as those used in the mixed ecosystem experiments. Destructive sampling was conducted at 0, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h to detect the amount of mineralization and distribution of 14C-CDs in water, C. pyrenoidosa, and D. magna. Polluted microalgae were supplemented every 24 h to an initial concentration of 1.0 × 106 cells/mL. Biological death was not observed in any of the three exposure pathways. Based on experimental results, the calculation method for the trophic transfer of 14C-CD is listed in SI.

The 14CO2 produced in the experiment was absorbed by the NaOH solution, which was measured using liquid scintillation counter (LSC) (LS-6500; Backman Coulter, USA) after mixing 2 mL of scintillation solution (Gold Star Multipurpose; Meridian Biotechnologies Ltd., UK). This represents the amount of mineralization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of radioactivity in water phase
ce:section.ce:para: The radioactivity in the water phase was measured as follows: after picking out D. magna and centrifuging and precipitating microalgae, the supernatant was used to determine radioactivity in water. The radioactivity of 10 mL of water was measured by LSC after mixing with a 10 mL of scintillation cocktail, and total radioactivity in the water phase was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of D. magna and microalgae samples
ce:section.ce:para: Daphnia magna was collected and transferred to deionized water overnight to clear gut contents. They were then extracted thrice using 2 mL of water (10 mL × 3) by ultrasound (sonic power of 60 %, time of 10 min, 2 s:2 s on/off). The supernatants were separated by centrifugation (4000 rpm, 10 min) and then combined. The extracts were determined using LSC after mixing them with a scintillation cocktail (1:1 v/v), which represented the water-extract residues (WERs). The precipitate was extracted thrice with methanol (10 mL) using the same method. The supernatant after centrifugation was analyzed using LSC, which represents methanol extract residues (MERs). Methanol-extracted D. magna was placed in a fume hood and dried. They were then combusted in a biological oxidizer for 4 min. The 14CO2 generated was absorbed by 15 mL of an alkaline scintillation cocktail and then determined using LSC, which represented non-extractable residues (NERs). After 96 h of exposure, the microalgae were separated by centrifugation (4000 rpm, 10 min) and detected using the abovementioned methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of D. magna for CDs exposure
ce:section.ce:para: Feeding behavior was selected to evaluate the response of D. magna to the CDs. Daphnia magna, aged 7 days, were used in a static system. The initial algal density was 1 × 106 cells/mL and the CD concentrations were set at 0.15, 1.5, and 15 ppm. Each group had five replicates and six D. magna samples were added in parallel. The culture conditions were the same as those used for the removal experiments. The experiment was performed in darkness to prevent algal growth and reproduction. The experiment lasted 96 h, and the medium and microalgae were replaced daily. For the waterborne exposure experiment, CDs were added to the culture medium, and pollution-free microalgae were used as food. For the dietary exposure experiment, the CDs were enriched in microalgae for 48 h in advance, and the culture medium was clean and pollution-free. At exposure times of 24, 48, 72, and 96 h, the algal density of C. pyrenoidosa was detected using optical microscopy to calculate the filtration and ingestion rates of D. magna following the methods used in our previous study (Yang et al., 2020).

Another batch of experiments was amplified ten times to culture D. magna. The operation followed the previously described method. After exposure for 96 h, D. magna was collected to determine the enzyme activities, gene expression, and symbiotic bacterial community. The analysis methods are listed in SII-SIV (supporting information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of C. pyrenoidosa for CDs exposure
ce:section.ce:para: The performance characteristics of the microalgae were monitored to analyze the effect of CDs on Daphnia food. Chlorella pyrenoidosa was exposed to non-labeled CDs at an initial algal density of 1 × 106 cells/mL and CD concentrations of 0.15, 1.5, and 15 ppm. The culture conditions were the same as those used in the previous experiments. Each group consisted of five replicates. The algae density and contents of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, and carotene were analyzed at 0, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h. The analysis methods are listed in SV (Supporting INFORMATION).

The range of radioactivity recoveries in all 14C treatment groups was 86.17 %–109.52 %, with an RSD of 5.98 %. All data were statistically analyzed using IBM SPSS 11.5 (SPSS, Inc., Chicago, IL, USA), and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was performed to evaluate the differences between groups. Significant differences (p < 0.05) are indicated using asterix (*) symbols. Data visualization was performed using Origin 6.0 software. Microbial diversity data analysis was performed using Trimmomatic 0.35, Flash 1.2.11, QIIME (split_libraries) 1.8.0, Vsearch 2.4.2, pynast 0.1, and QIIME (multiple_rarefactions.py) 1.8.0.
14C-CDs of approximately 40 nm were selected as representatives to study the bioaccumulation and distribution of CDs in the microalgae/D. magna mixed ecosystem.

The fate and distribution of carbon elements in the CDs were traced using 14C tracer technique (Figs. 1A-C). The 14C‑carbon of CDs was typically transferred from water to organisms. Mineralization is a process by which pollutants are completely converted into CO2. Although the amount of mineralization of the CDs was small, the amount of mineralization increased with an increase in the exposure time. After 96 h of exposure, the final mineralization amounts were 2.7 ± 0.2 %, 3.2 ± 0.7 %, and 3.0 ± 0.02 % of the initial radioactivity in the 0.15, 1.5, and 15 mg/L CD groups, respectively. The amount of mineralization of CDs in the control group (without algae or D. magna) was <1 %, which is not shown in the figure. These results suggest that the microalgae/D. magna mixed ecosystem plays a significant role in promoting the mineralization of CDs. Radioactivity in the water phase decreased after 24 h of exposure, following which no significant changes were observed. After 96 h of exposure, radioactivity in water was 86.7 ± 0.3 %, 83.9 ± 0.2 %, and 84.1 ± 1.3 % in the 0.15, 1.5, and 15 mg/L CD groups, respectively. Residual radioactivity was enriched in C. pyrenoidosa and D. magna. There was no significant difference in the distribution of 14C‑carbon in water among the three concentration groups.

To analyze the 14C‑carbon forms in C. pyrenoidosa and D. magna, the radioactive substances in C. pyrenoidosa and D. magna were extracted with water and methanol, which represent the water-extract residues (WERs) and methanol extract residues (MERs), respectively. After 96 h of exposure, the radioactivity of C. pyrenoidosa WERs was 4.2 ± 0.4 %, 6.1 ± 0.4 %, and 7.4 ± 1.2 % in the 0.15, 1.5, and 15 mg/L CD groups, respectively, while that of D. magna WERs was 5.4 ± 0.04 %, 5.9 ± 0.8 %, and 4.6 ± 0.2 %, respectively. The radioactivity of MERs and non-extractable residues (NERs) in C. pyrenoidosa and D. magna was <0.5 %. These results indicate that the carbon from the CDs still has strong water solubility and exists primarily in water and biological water extracts. The formation of NERs can reduce the re-release of the pollutants into the environment, thereby reducing the bioavailability, toxicity, and volatility of the pollutants to other organisms, which can be considered as the detoxification process of CDs of the environment and organisms (Zeng et al., 2018; Jie et al., 2014). However, the amounts of NERs in C. pyrenoidosa and D. magna are small and potential risks to the environment should be considered.

Using the 15 mg/L CD group as an example, the accumulation of 14C-CDs in microalgae was studied, and the results are shown in Fig. 1D. After 96 h of exposure, the mineralization amount was 2.0 ± 0.1 % of the initial radioactivity. Radioactivity in the water phase decreased after 96 h of exposure, at which time the radioactivity in water was 81.6 ± 1.8 %, whereas that in C. pyrenoidosa it was 16.3 ± 1.9 %. 14C‑carbon from CDs was continuously enriched by C. pyrenoidosa, which differed from the trend of algae-enriched organic pollutants (first increasing and then decreasing) (Liu et al., 2019b). In algae, the radioactivity of WERs, MERs, and NERs was 14.7 ± 1.8 %, 0.8 ± 0.03 %, and 0.7 ± 0.2 %, respectively. The range of BCF values for the total radioactivity in the microalgae was 0.0087–0.1675 during 48 h of exposure (Fig. S2). These results indicate that the carbon from CDs still has strong water solubility, which is similar to the results of the exposure experiments in the C. pyrenoidosa/D. magna mixed ecosystem.

Fig. 1E shows the accumulation and distribution of 14C‑carbon from CDs by D. magna via the aqueous route. After 96 h of exposure, 3.4 % ± 1.1 % of the initial radioactivity was mineralized, which was significantly higher than that in the microalgal exposure experiment (Fig. 1D). However, 95.1 % ± 1.1 % of the initial radioactivity was trapped in water and only 1.4 ± 0.05 % was enriched by D. magna. We speculated that the removal of pollutants primarily occurs in the gut of D. magna (Hoffschrer et al., 2021; Magro et al., 2018), which leads to easier excretion of metabolites. In D. magna, the radioactivity of WERs, MERs, and NERS was 1.1 % ± 0.2 %, 0.1 % ± 0.01 %, and 0.2 % ± 0.02 %, respectively. As expected, WERs were also the dominant 14C‑carbon in D. magna. The range of BCF values for the total radioactivity in D. magna was 0.0016–0.0175 during 96 h of exposure (Fig. S2).

Fig. 1F shows the accumulation and distribution of 14C‑carbon from CDs during 96 h of treatment with C. pyrenoidosa-D. magna via the dietary route. D. magna were fed 1.0 × 106 cells/mL of polluted C. pyrenoidosa, and the solution was pollutant free. After 24 h of feeding, the radioactivity in the water increased rapidly to 39.9 ± 3.4 % of the initial radioactivity in the algae and then increased to 54.3 ± 1.0 % after 96 h. The radioactivity of the algal residue decreased significantly. This may be because the 14C‑carbon of CDs enriched in algal cells was released into the water after digestion by D. magna. It is also possible that some CDs were attached to the surface of algal cells by adsorption, and that they might have entered the water phase directly. After 24 h of feeding, the algal WERs, MERs, and NERs were significantly decreased. This finding suggests that extractable and bound residues are released after the intestinal digestion of D. magna. This result was similar to our previous research results, wherein we found that the bound residues in algae become extractable residues after the intestinal digestion of rotifers, and are then released into water (Guo et al., 2017). However, Navarro et al. (2012) found that the bound residues in Lumbriculus variegatus were not used by its predator Salmo trutta, but in the excreta of young fish. Therefore, the form of carbon entering the CDs in water and its bioavailability require further study. Additionally, the accumulated radioactivity in the algae decreased by 85 % within 24 h of feeding, and that in D. magna reached 44 %. These results show that D. magna can accumulate pollutants and/or their residues through the feeding phase. Extractable and bound residues were detected in D. magna. The WERs in D. magna peaked at 24 h and then decreased significantly (p < 0.05). The MERs increased from 0 to 48 h, peaked at 48 h (3.2 ± 0.8 %), and then decreased. NERs increased from 0 to 72 h, peaked at 72 h (4.1 ± 0.1 %), and then decreased. BMF values of CD in the “C. pyrenoidosa-D. magna” food chain ranged from 0.85 to 3.75, indicating biomagnification (Fig. S2). Based on the changes in extractable and bound residues, we speculated that the decrease in extractable radioactivity over time was due to the elimination of D. magna itself, and that the bound radioactivity was not easily eliminated, which increased the possibility of carbon transfer at the trophic level (Guo et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2018).

Ultrastructural changes in microalgae and Daphnia were identified using TEM, the results of which are shown in Fig. 2 . As shown in Fig. 2A, the algal cells in the control group were small and round, with intact cell walls. In the group exposed to 0.15 mg/L of CDs, the microalgal cells were damaged and wall-membrane detachment was observed (Fig. 2B). In the group exposed to 1.5 mg/L of CDs, wall membrane detachment and cell collapse were observed in exposed algae (Fig. 2C). Cell rupture was occasionally observed in the group treated with 15 mg/L of CDs (Fig. 2D). The damage to algal cells increased with increasing CD concentration, which was similar to a previous study (Zhang et al., 2020b). The uptake of CDs may be toxic to microalgae.

Daphnia magna ingests CDs through filtration and feeding, and the intestinal tract is the first step for D. magna to contact CDs. The ultrastructural changes in the Daphnia intestinal tract are shown in Figs. 2E–H. In the control group, the intestinal structure of D. magna was intact, without any suspected black CD aggregates. There were clear CD aggregates in all exposed groups, which indicated that Daphnia enriched CDs, and these results are similar to those of Chen et al. (2022). CDs accumulated in the intestine and liver of carp after 35 days of exposure to 2 mg/L and 20 mg/L CDs solutions and caused oxidative damage. Additionally, 1.5-and 15 mg/L of CDs caused pneumatosis cystoides intestinalis (Fig. 2G and H), which indicated that CDs caused damage to the intestine of D. magna. Whether this affects the feeding behavior of D. magna is discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Responses of D. magna to CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Daphnia magna are sensitive to pollutants (Dimitri et al., 2018). Therefore, analyzing the response of D. magna to CDs was helpful in assessing the environmental risk of CDs.

Fig. 3 shows the feeding behavior of D. magna exposed to CDs via aqueous and dietary routes. For the aqueous route, the filtration rate of D. magna increased until 72 h of exposure (Fig. 3A). The increase in filtration rate increased the intake of pollutants by D. magna. The ingestion rate of D. magna increased in the 15 mg/L CD group at 24 and 48 h and in the 1.5 mg/L CD group at 96 h (Fig. 3B). The increase in feeding behavior increases the energy intake of D. magna, which can enhance the physiological function of D. magna to better adapt to the environment (Wang et al., 2019). The decrease in filtration and ingestion rates of D. magna at 72 h may be due to detoxification. For the dietary route, the filtration rate of D. magna increased in the 15 mg/L CD group at 24 h and deceased in the 1.5 mg/L CD group at 72 h and 96 h (Fig. 3C). The ingestion rate of D. magna increased in the 0.15 mg/L CD group at 24 h and 96 h and the 15 mg/L CD group at 24 h and decreased in the 1.5 mg/L CD group at 72 h and 96 h (Fig. 3D). Similar to our previous results, the feeding behavior of D. magna caused by azithromycin via aqueous and dietary routes was different (Yang et al., 2020). CDs are toxic to algae and D. magna in a concentration- and route-dependent manner.

Food changes may also affect the feeding behavior of D. magna. The response of microalgae to the CDs is shown in Fig. S3 (supporting information). Except for the 15 mg/L CD group, the algal density in the experimental groups showed an upward trend. In the 15 mg/L CD group, the algal density increased from 0 to 72 h of exposure and then decreased after 96 h of exposure. Compared to the control group, the specific microalgal growth rate in the experimental groups fluctuated after 96 h of CDs exposure. The specific microalgal growth rate in the experimental groups was significantly higher than that in the control group at 24 h and 72 h, and significantly lower at 48 h and 96 h. Previous studies also found that exposure to low CD concentrations (≤ 10 mg/L) or short exposure time periods (≤ 24 h) increased algal growth, whereas those with high CD concentrations or longer exposure periods significantly reduced algal growth (Yao et al., 2018; Xiao et al., 2016a, 2016b).

The chlorophyll content represents photosynthetic activity (Babadi et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. S3, with an increase in exposure time, the contents of chlorophyll a, chlorophyll b, and carotene in the algae increased. After 96 h, the chlorophyll content of algae exposed to medium and high CD concentrations was higher than that in the control group. These results may be due to the hormesis of CDs on algae (Agathokleous et al., 2019). The unit content of chlorophyll in the exposed algae showed fluctuating growth, which was similar to the microalgal growth rate. These results may be attributed to the toxicity of CDs to microalgae and the recovery ability of the microalgae. Xiao et al. (2016a, 2016b) reported that the 96 h EC50 of CDs for C. pyrenoidosa was 232.47 mg/L. The damage caused by CDs (under 15 mg/L) to algae can be offset by the algal recovery ability when CD concentrations are relatively low.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Damage in oxidative stress and the digestive system
ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme activity and gene transcription are the bases for assessing the response of D. magna to pollutants (Li et al., 2020). The activities and gene transcription of SOD, CAT, lipase, trypsin, and α-amylase in exposed D. magna were evaluated to demonstrate the effects of CDs on the oxidative stress and digestive systems of D. magna.

Fig. 4A and B show the enzyme activity and gene transcription of SOD and CAT in D. magna. For the aqueous route, SOD activity in D. magna exposed to 1.5 mg/L CDs increased significantly, whereas that in D. magna exposed to 15 mg/L CDs decreased significantly. SOD is an antioxidant enzyme that protects organisms from oxidative damage by triggering the dismutation of superoxide radicals (O2 −) into hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and oxygen (O2) (Sivula et al., 2018). The increase in SOD activity indicated that 1.5 mg/L CDs induced oxidative damage in D. magna. However, the high stress caused by high concentrations of CDs can destroy the antioxidant system of D. magna and inhibit the activity of oxidative stress enzymes (Feng et al., 2017). Regarding the dietary route, SOD activity in D. magna exposed to 1.5 and 15 mg/L CDs increased significantly. Compared to the aqueous route, the transfer of CDs via the dietary route caused less oxidative damage to D. magna. There was no significant difference in the CAT activity between the experimental groups. However, the trend in gene transcription of SOD and CAT was irreconcilable with the corresponding enzyme activity (Fig. 4B). These results indicate an unknown complex mechanism for the risk of CDs in D. magna. Daphnia magna is exceedingly complex and more studies are required to understand its response to CDs.

Fig. 4C and D show the enzyme activity and gene transcription of lipase, trypsin, and α-amylase, respectively. Lipase, trypsin, and α-amylase are enzymes involved in fat, protein, and starch digestion, respectively (Yang et al., 2020). The lipase activity in D. magna exposed to 15 mg/L CDs via the dietary route increased significantly (p < 0.05), indicating that the fat nitrification ability of D. magna was significantly enhanced. For trypsin, the enzyme activity in all exposed D. magna decreased significantly (p < 0.05, p < 0.01), indicating that CDs could damage the protein digestion function of D. magna via both exposure routes. The activity of α-amylase in D. magna exposed to 15 mg/L CDs via the aqueous route increased significantly (p < 0.05). Damage to the digestive system caused by CDs via the dietary route was significantly lower than that caused via the aqueous route. Therefore, the microalgae/D. magna mixed ecosystems can effectively alleviate the negative impact of CDs on D. magna, which is conducive to the popularization and application of this biotechnology. Additionally, gene expression of lipase, trypsin, and α-amylase in D. magna was widely affected by CDs, and there were no significant relationships between the activity and gene expression levels of the three enzymes. These results suggest that CDs have complex impacts on the production and transport of digestive enzymes in D. magna (Zhao et al., 2019).

The mutualistic microbiota of D. magna plays a vital role in the physiological functions of Daphnia and pollutant outcomes (Callens et al., 2016). CDs exhibit strong antibacterial effects (Nie et al., 2020). During treatment with CDs, the mutualistic microbiota of D. magna may be affected to some extent. As shown in Fig. 5A, the bacterial phyla inhabiting exposed and non-exposed D. magna mostly overlapped; however, the abundance of these phyla differed greatly. The major phyla of the mutualistic microbiota are Proteobacteria and Bacteroidetes, which together constitute >88 % of the total microbiota. In the control group, the relative abundance of Proteobacteria and Bacteroidetes was 57.3 % and 42.2 %, respectively. For Daphnia exposed via the aqueous route, the relative abundance of Proteobacteria increased (61.8 %, 68.2 %, and 66.9 % for the 0.15 mg/L, 1.5 mg/L, and 15 mg/L groups, respectively) and that of Bacteroidetes decreased (37.6 %, 30.6 %, and 32.2 % for the 0.15 mg/L, 1.5 mg/L, and 15 mg/L groups, respectively). For Daphnia exposed via the dietary route, the relative abundance of Proteobacteria increased in the 0.15 mg/L (60.4 %) and 15 mg/L groups (69.6 %), and that of Bacteroidetes decreased in all exposed groups (38.8 %, 33.7 %, and 28.3 % for the 0.15 mg/L, 1.5 mg/L group, and 15 mg/L groups, respectively). The relative abundance of Proteobacteria and Bacteroidetes decreased in the 1.5 mg/L group via the dietary route, and that of Firmicutes increased significantly (10.3 %). The differences between Proteobacteria, Bacteroidetes, and Firmicutes include the structure of the cell wall and oxygen tolerance (Zhang et al., 2020c). Bacteroidetes and Firmicutes are strictly anaerobic, whereas Proteobacteria includes aerobic or facultative anaerobic genera. The increase in Proteobacteria abundance and decrease in Bacteroidetes abundance indicated that the CDs increased the oxygen levels in D. magna. However, the high Firmicutes abundance in the 1.5 mg/L group via the dietary route was puzzling, which may be related to diet (Mushegian and Ebert, 2017).

Fig. 5B shows the taxonomic classification of the mutualistic microbiota at the genus level. The abundance of Emticicia, Acidovorax, and Zoogloea increased in the three groups exposed via the aqueous route, whereas that of Acinetobacter decreased. For the 0.15 mg/L and 15 mg/L groups exposed via the dietary route, the abundance of Emticicia, Acidovorax, Methylophilus, Acinetobacter, and Bacteroides increased. For the 1.5 mg/L groups exposed via the dietary route, the abundance of Lachnospiraceae_NK4A136_group, Emticicia, Prevotellaceae_UCG-001, Alloprevotella, Pantoea, Acinetobacter, and Bacteroides increased, whereas that of Zoogloea and Methylophilus decreased. These results indicate that CDs significantly affect the structure of the microbial community, similar to the results from previous studies (Nie et al., 2020; Sun et al., 2021). The bacteriostatic effect of CDs also affects the normal symbiotic flora of aquatic organisms, endangers the health of aquatic organisms, and further affects the stability of aquatic ecosystems.

CDs are a category of novel carbon nanomaterials with excellent physicochemical properties and wide range of applications. However, the ecological risks posed by CDs in aquatic environments have recently been identified. In this study, from the perspective of the “Chlorella pyrenoidosa - Daphnia magna” aquatic system, the uptake of CDs by aquatic organisms and the potential risk of CDs were traced. During 96 h of exposure, most of the radioactivity was distributed in water (80 % of the initial radioactivity), and the mineralization of CDs was negligible (<6 %). Radioactivity in microalgae and D. magna existed primarily in the water extract residues. According to the results of ultrastructural investigations of microalgae and D. magna, CD aggregates were found in algal cells and Daphnia intestinal tract with evident cell damage. To investigate the effects of different internalization routes on the environmental fate and risk of CDs, the treatment pathway was divided into two aqueous routes and one dietary route. Similar to the results obtained for microalgae/D. magna mixed ecosystem treatment experiments, 14C‑carbon from CDs was distributed mainly in the water and WERs of the organisms. The changes in extractable and bound residues indicate the possibility of carbon transfer at the trophic level. By observing the growth and chlorophyll content in exposed algae, and the feeding, enzyme activity, gene expression, and mutualistic microbiota in exposed D. magna, the risk of CDs to the organisms was evaluated. The deterioration of algal growth, chlorophyll content, the feeding behavior, oxidative stress system, digestive system, and symbiotic bacteria of D. magna may be related to the accumulation of CDs.

Yanhua Liu: carried out experiments and wrote the manuscript; Yunfeng Ma: carried out experiments and analyzed experimental results; Meilin Chen and Tianhan Zhou: developed the methodology; Rong Ji and Ruixin Guo: analyzed experimental results; Jianqiu Chen: managed and coordinated responsibility for the research activity planning and execution.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21876207), Double First-Class University Project (CPU2018GY21, 24), and College Undergraduate Training Program for Innovation and Entrepreneurship.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2022.157201.
